content.problemStatement:
What is a Linked List?



Imagine you are given a task where you have to maintain a data entry of cars entering a parking lot. Since the number of cars entering will be different and may change daily, constructing a **fixed-sized data structure** like an array might not be helpful. This is when linked lists come into the picture, which allows us to add and remove cars easily. Linked List is a linear data structure that can be visualized as a chain with different nodes connected, where each node represents a different element. The difference between arrays and linked lists is that, unlike arrays, the elements are not stored at a contiguous location.




<!-- wp:paragraph
content.inputFormat: The input will contain an array of integers representing the data of the nodes in the linked list.
content.outputFormat: The output will be the memory location of the first node and the data stored in the first node.

hidden.label: min boundary
hidden.expectedOutput: Memory location of the first node and the data stored in the first node.

hidden.label: max boundary
hidden.expectedOutput: Memory location of the first node and the data stored in the first node.

hidden.label: uniform/edge cases
hidden.expectedOutput: Memory location of the first node and the data stored in the first node.

hidden.label: tricky case / empty-like / negative if allowed
general mixed case
hidden.expectedOutput: Memory location of the first node and the data stored in the first node.

solution.approach: Create a Node class to represent each node in the linked list. Each node will have a data value and a pointer to the next node.

code.Python3:
# Node class represents a node in the linked list
class Node:
    def __init__(self, data, next=None):
        self.data = data      # Data value
        self.next = next      # Pointer to next node

# Driver code
if __name__ == "__main__":
    # Create an array
    arr = [2, 5, 8, 7]

    # Create first node
    y = Node(arr[0])

    # Print memory reference of node
    print(y)

    # Print data stored in node
    print(y.data)
